participants.extracted: Adults aged 18 to 70 years with a diagnosis of type 1 or type 2 diabetes for at least 3 months, who had at least mild depressive symptoms (BDI-II score ≥ 14) and were recruited from diabetes outpatient clinics in participating Dutch hospitals or by self-referral. Patients had to be able to read and write Dutch and provide informed consent. Exclusion criteria included pregnancy, severe psychiatric comorbidity (recent psychosis, bipolar disorder, panic disorder, schizophrenia, or serious cognitive/neurological problems), acute suicidal ideation or behavior, and concurrent psychological treatment or psychological treatment within the prior 2 months. Stable antidepressant use was permitted if the regimen had been unchanged for at least 2 months before enrollment.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to one of two active psychological treatments: individual cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) or individual mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT), each delivered in eight weekly face-to-face sessions lasting 45 to 60 minutes, with a workbook and daily homework assignments of about 30 minutes; MBCT participants also received audio CDs with mindfulness exercises. CBT was based on Beck’s depression protocol, beginning with behavioral activation (e.g., planning and doing pleasant or functional activities) and followed by identifying, challenging, and modifying dysfunctional automatic thoughts. MBCT was based on the Segal et al. protocol, adapted from the standard group format to an individualized version; it combined cognitive therapy for depression with mindful meditation to help patients cultivate an open, accepting awareness of negative thoughts, feelings, and bodily sensations, with later sessions including specific relapse-prevention strategies. Therapists were trained and supervised, and patients could discuss diabetes-related issues when relevant to their mood.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure depressive symptoms as the primary outcome using the Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) at baseline (T1), after sessions 1, 2, and 4 (T2–T4), post-treatment after session 8 (T5), and follow-up at 3 months (T6) and 9 months (T7); depressive symptoms were also assessed by interviewer-rated 7-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D7) at baseline (T1) and post-treatment (T5). Secondary psychological outcomes included generalized anxiety measured by the Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD-7) at T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6, and T7; well-being measured by the WHO-5 Well-being Index at the same time points; diabetes-related distress measured by the Problem Areas in Diabetes scale (PAID) at the same time points; and intersession mood changes measured at the start of every treatment session using the Emotion Thermometers Tool (ETT). Medical secondary outcome was glycemic control measured by HbA1c retrieved from patient records, using values averaged over the 6 months before intervention as the pre-treatment measure and the 6 months after intervention as the post-treatment measure. The study also planned to measure potential moderators, including neuroticism using the NEO-Five Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) and attachment style using the Experiences in Close Relationships Scale short form (ECR-S) at baseline, history of depression by Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-IV (SCID-I) at baseline, demographic and diabetes-specific characteristics from medical records at baseline, and degradation of tryptophan from blood samples at baseline. Planned mediators were cognitive coping (positive re-interpretation and positive refocusing on the CERQ; reappraisal and distraction on the TCQ), behavioral activation (Behavioral Activation for Depression Scale, BADS), rumination (Rumination-Reflection Questionnaire, RRQ), mindfulness (non-judgmental attitude and act with awareness on the Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire, FFMQ), attention control (Self-Regulation Scale), and self-compassion (self-kindness, self-judgment, and overidentification on the Self Compassion Scale, SCS), assessed repeatedly during treatment across T1–T5. Common factors were therapeutic alliance measured with the Working Alliance Inventory-12 (WAI-12) and the 18-item Rapport Questionnaire at T2 and T4, and treatment expectancy measured with a 3-item credibility questionnaire at baseline (T1).
